title: System Design Roadmap
description: Master system design from fundamentals to designing large-scale distributed systems for interviews and real-world applications.

topics.title: What is System Design?
topics.description: System design is the process of defining the architecture, components, modules, interfaces, and data for a system to satisfy specified requirements. It involves making high-level decisions about how different parts of a system interact.

interviewQuestions.a: The process of defining architecture and modules for a scalable system.

topics.title: How to approach System Design?
topics.description: A systematic approach to solving system design problems includes gathering requirements, estimating scale, designing the high-level architecture, diving into core components, and addressing bottlenecks.

youtubeVideos.title: How to approach System Design Interviews

interviewQuestions.q: What are the steps in a system design interview?
interviewQuestions.a: Understand requirements, capacity planning, high level design, low level design, bottlenecks.

tips: Never jump straight to the architecture without clarifying functional and non-functional requirements.

topics.title: Performance vs Scalability
topics.description: A service is scalable if it results in increased performance in a manner proportional to resources added.

interviewQuestions.a: Performance is about a single user's experience (speed). Scalability is maintaining performance as users increase.

tips: Always design for scalability, but optimize for performance when bottlenecks become apparent.

topics.title: Latency vs Throughput
topics.description: Latency is the time to perform some action or to produce some result. Throughput is the number of such actions or results per unit of time.

interviewQuestions.a: By increasing parallelism, adding cache, horizontally scaling the servers.

tips: Aim for maximum throughput with acceptable latency in most web systems.

topics.title: Availability vs Consistency (CAP Theorem)
topics.description: CAP theorem states that a distributed system cannot simultaneously be consistent, available, and partition tolerant (choose 2).

resources.title: An Illustrated Proof of CAP Theorem

interviewQuestions.q: What happens in a network partition for a CP system?
interviewQuestions.a: The system denies requests (becomes unavailable) to keep the data consistent across nodes.

tips: Most major cloud apps choose AP or eventual consistency.

topics.title: Consistency Patterns
topics.description: Strong, eventual, and weak consistency patterns.

interviewQuestions.a: When high availability and low latency are more important than absolute correctness right away (e.g. social media feeds).

tips: Always clarify consistency models when discussing DBs.

topics.title: Availability Patterns

interviewQuestions.a: Traffic goes to multiple nodes at the same time to ensure no single point of failure.

tips: Redundancy is the mathematical key to high availability.

topics.title: Domain Name System (DNS)
topics.description: DNS maps domain names to IP addresses.

interviewQuestions.a: Browser cache -> OS cache -> Resolver -> Root server -> TLD server -> Authoritative server.

tips: Mention DNS routing policies (Geographic, Weighted) in global designs.

topics.title: Content Delivery Networks (CDNs)
topics.description: Globally distributed network of proxy servers for fast delivery of assets.

interviewQuestions.a: Pull: CDN requests from origin when cache misses. Push: You actively upload to CDN.

tips: Always use CDNs for static assets like images, JS, CSS in your architecture diagrams.

topics.title: Load Balancers
topics.description: Distributes incoming network traffic across a group of backend servers.

interviewQuestions.a: L4 routes based on IP info (TCP/UDP). L7 routes based on HTTP headers/content.

tips: Understand Round Robin vs Least Connections.

topics.title: Application Layer
topics.description: Monoliths vs Microservices, and Service Discovery.

interviewQuestions.q: How does service discovery work in microservices?
interviewQuestions.a: Using a unified registry (like Consul or Eureka) where instances register their IPs for dynamic routing.

tips: Always justify why you chose microservices over a well-designed modular monolith.

topics.title: Asynchronism
topics.description: Back pressure, task queues, and message queues allow decouple components.

interviewQuestions.q: When to use Kafka over RabbitMQ?
interviewQuestions.a: Kafka is best for high throughput event streaming and log aggregation. RabbitMQ is best for complex message routing.

tips: Use queues to handle traffic spikes and build reliable asynchronous workflows.

topics.title: Communication Protocols
topics.description: HTTP, TCP, UDP, RPC, REST, gRPC, and GraphQL.

interviewQuestions.a: gRPC uses HTTP/2 and Protobuf for multiplexing and smaller binary payloads, making inter-microservice communication much faster.

tips: Choose REST for public APIs and gRPC for internal service-to-service communication.

topics.title: Databases (SQL vs NoSQL)
topics.description: RDBMS (Replication, Sharding, Denormalization) and NoSQL (Key-Value, Document, Wide Column, Graph).

interviewQuestions.a: Partitioning divides a table into multiple parts. Sharding places these parts on entirely different physical database servers.

tips: Sharding should be your last resort due to cross-shard join complexity.

topics.title: Caching
topics.description: Strategies (Write-through, Write-behind, Cache Aside) and Types (CDN, DB, App).

interviewQuestions.q: What is cache stampede and how do you prevent it?
interviewQuestions.a: When a hot key expires and concurrent requests hit the DB simultaneously. Fixed by using a distributed lock.

tips: Cache eviction policies (LRU, LFU) are common interview questions.

topics.title: Performance Antipatterns
topics.description: Common pitfalls like Busy Database, Chatty I/O, Monolithic Persistence, and Noisy Neighbor.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Noisy Neighbor problem?
interviewQuestions.a: In a multi-tenant environment, one tenant uses a massive amount of shared resources, starving others.

tips: Always plan for multi-tenancy throttling in SaaS architectures.

topics.title: Monitoring
topics.description: Health, Availability, Performance, Security metrics, Logging, and Alerting.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the 4 golden signals of monitoring?

tips: Always include a monitoring layer (e.g. Prometheus + Grafana/ELK) in your diagrams.

topics.title: Data Management & Messaging Patterns
topics.description: Event Sourcing, CQRS, Materialized View, Sharding, Publisher/Subscriber.

interviewQuestions.a: Command Query Responsibility Segregation separates read and update operations for a data store to maximize performance, scalability, and security.

tips: Only use Event Sourcing when you absolutely need a strict audit trail or playback functionality.

topics.title: Reliability & Resiliency
topics.description: Circuit Breaker, Bulkhead, Throttling, Leader Election, Sidecar, Strangler Fig.

interviewQuestions.q: How does the Circuit Breaker pattern work?
interviewQuestions.a: It intercepts requests to a failing service. If failures cross a threshold, it 'trips', immediately failing subsequent requests to prevent overloading the broken service.

tips: Mention retries with exponential backoff and jitter alongside Circuit Breakers.
